scenes.narration_text: Imagine, if you will, that one single building in the middle of a Dutch field just… stopped. The world wouldn't just be slightly annoyed; it would end. No new iPhones, no AI, no guided missiles. Nothing. This isn't a Bond villain's plot; it's a company called ASML. And they are the most important people you've never heard of.

data.heading: One Building. One Company.
data.body: If it stops, the world stops with it.

scenes.narration_text: Now, you might have noticed their stock price. In early 2026, it hit fifteen hundred euros. To the casual observer, it looks like a lucky break — an overnight success. But at 'Luck vs. Boring,' we know that's rubbish. This isn't luck. It's a forty-year, bloody-minded obsession with a very expensive lightbulb.

data.subtitle: From underdog to €1,500 — forty years in the making

scenes.narration_text: But what exactly does ASML do that makes them so irreplaceable? They build the only machines on Earth capable of printing the most advanced microchips. Every cutting-edge processor in your phone, your laptop, and every AI data centre on the planet was made using an ASML machine. No ASML, no chips. No chips, no modern economy. It's that simple.

To understand their power, you have to look at the physics — which is, frankly, mental. They take a tiny drop of molten tin and hit it with a laser fifty thousand times every single second. This creates a plasma forty times hotter than the surface of the sun.

data.label: Laser Pulses Per Second
data.subtitle: A molten tin droplet, vaporized at impossible speed.

scenes.narration_text: It's basically a star in a box, just to print a microchip. The plasma reaches two hundred and twenty thousand degrees Celsius — and only about two percent of that energy actually converts into usable light. The rest is waste heat that has to be managed in real time. The engineering precision here is measured in atoms, not millimetres.

data.label: Plasma Temperature
data.subtitle: 40x hotter than the surface of the sun. 2% energy conversion.

scenes.narration_text: This isn't just a business; it's a monopoly on the future. Their machines cost three hundred and eighty million dollars each. Intel and Samsung are currently standing in a very expensive queue, waiting for ASML's permission to exist. If you want to build the future, you have to ask these Dutchmen nicely.

data.label: Cost Per High-NA EUV System
data.subtitle: Intel and Samsung are in the queue.

scenes.narration_text: Let's put that price tag in perspective. At three hundred and eighty million dollars, one ASML machine costs more than a Boeing 787 Dreamliner. More than an F-35 fighter jet. And unlike those, there is no alternative supplier. You either buy from ASML, or you don't make advanced chips. Full stop.

data.subtitle: One lithography machine costs more than a commercial airliner.

scenes.narration_text: The customers lining up for these machines read like a who's who of the semiconductor world. TSMC, the world's largest chipmaker, takes the lion's share. Then Intel, Samsung, and SK Hynix. Each one is betting billions on ASML's technology to stay competitive in the AI race.

data.subtitle: Every advanced chipmaker on Earth depends on ASML.

scenes.narration_text: And the AI boom is only accelerating this demand. Every major tech company — Microsoft, Google, Meta, Amazon — is spending tens of billions on new data centres. Every single one of those data centres needs chips. And every single one of those chips needs an ASML machine to exist. The capex cycle is feeding directly into ASML's order book.

data.heading: AI Capex Explosion
data.body: Every data centre needs chips. Every chip needs ASML.

scenes.narration_text: But even a giant has a glass jaw: Geopolitics.

Back in October 2024, the world realized how fragile this was. In one afternoon, ASML's stock plummeted sixteen percent — erasing fifty billion euros. The most important company you've never heard of suddenly became front-page news. Why? Because an IT bloke accidentally leaked their report early, and everyone realized China was about to be cut off.

At that time, China was buying nearly half of everything ASML made. When Washington and Beijing started shouting at each other, ASML got hit in the face. Nearly forty-seven percent of their revenue was coming from a single geopolitical flashpoint. That's not a business risk; that's a ticking time bomb.

data.subtitle: Nearly half of all ASML sales went to China before the ban.

scenes.narration_text: The export restrictions didn't happen overnight. It started quietly in 2019 when the Dutch government delayed an export license for China's SMIC. Then in 2022, the US imposed sweeping chip controls. By January 2024, the Netherlands formally banned EUV exports to China. Each step tightened the noose, and ASML was caught in the middle.

data.title: Five Years of Tightening Controls
bullets: 2019: Dutch gov delays SMIC export license

scenes.narration_text: To the 'lucky' investor, an absolute disaster. To the 'boring' investor, a once-in-a-decade discount on a monopoly.

data.statement: A disaster for the lucky. A discount for the boring.

scenes.narration_text: Because the boring investor asks one simple question: does the underlying technology monopoly change because of a political headline? And the answer, every single time, is no. The physics doesn't care about politics. The monopoly doesn't care about headlines.

data.heading: Does the Monopoly Change?
data.body: Political headlines come and go. Physics doesn't.

scenes.narration_text: And look what happened next. By 2025, they hit record sales of nearly thirty-three billion euros. Turns out, the world needs AI more than it needs political posturing. The revenue didn't just recover; it went into orbit. Despite losing nearly half their China business, global AI demand more than filled the gap.

data.subtitle: Record €33B in 2025 — AI demand overwhelmed the China ban

scenes.narration_text: The stock told the same story. After the October crash, it didn't just bounce back — it climbed right back and then some. The boring investors who bought the dip were rewarded handsomely. The market remembered what matters: ASML has no competition, and AI isn't slowing down.

data.subtitle: The boring investor's reward: buying a monopoly on sale

scenes.narration_text: When your backlog is forty billion euros, short-term news is just… noise. That backlog is the real story. ASML's production is completely sold out through 2027. Customers are queuing years in advance for machines that haven't even been built yet.

data.subtitle: Sold out through 2027 — years of guaranteed revenue.

scenes.narration_text: But why are they so dominant? Simple. Persistence.

In 1984, they started in a leaky shed behind a Philips factory with a hundred people and no money. By 1988, they were nearly bankrupt. Most companies would have folded. Most investors would have walked away. But ASML stuck to their guns and kept going.

They stuck to their guns for thirty years while giants like Canon and Nikon eventually just… gave up. Because they were bored. Canon retreated to older, cheaper nodes. Nikon invested heavily in their own EUV research but the technical gaps were too wide. By 2020, Nikon's semiconductor lithography revenue had collapsed. ASML wasn't bored. ASML was obsessed.

data.heading: The Competition Quit
data.body: Canon retreated. Nikon collapsed. ASML was obsessed.

scenes.narration_text: Canon and Nikon bet on older lithography technology — deep ultraviolet light at 193 nanometres. It worked brilliantly for a while, but it hit a physics wall below seven nanometres. ASML pushed through to extreme ultraviolet at 13.5 nanometres. The others gave up. ASML didn't. That's the difference between luck and boring.

The stock performance tells the whole story. While ASML has been on a relentless climb over the past five years, Nikon's semiconductor business has flatlined. This is what happens when persistence meets physics. The boring company wins. Every single time.

data.subtitle: Persistence vs. giving up — reflected in stock performance

scenes.narration_text: Today, ASML controls one hundred percent of the EUV market and over eighty percent of all advanced lithography. Nikon and Canon are left fighting over the remaining scraps of older DUV systems. The race is over. ASML won it by refusing to quit when everyone else decided the physics was simply impossible.

data.subtitle: 100% of EUV, 82% of total advanced lithography.

scenes.narration_text: So, here's the verdict. ASML is at its peak, and it's priced for perfection. With a P/E ratio over fifty, it trades at nearly double the semiconductor equipment peer average. But here's the thing: none of those peers have a monopoly. None of them have a forty-billion-euro backlog. And none of them are irreplaceable.

data.subtitle: ASML trades at nearly 2x the peer average P/E.

scenes.narration_text: Let's see how ASML stacks up against those peers in the market over the past year. The semiconductor equipment sector has had a very strong run, but ASML's monopoly position gives it a different risk profile entirely. When you're the only game in town, the rules are different.

data.subtitle: ASML vs KLA, Lam Research, Applied Materials — 1 year

scenes.narration_text: And when you look at revenue growth, the gap becomes even clearer. ASML grew over fifteen percent in 2025 while most peers grew in the single digits. The AI capex cycle is funnelling billions directly into ASML's order book, and that trend shows no signs of slowing down.

data.subtitle: AI capex cycle drives ASML's outperformance.

scenes.narration_text: The premium is steep, but the fundamentals are unlike anything else in the semiconductor supply chain. There is no second source. There is no alternative. And the demand curve for advanced chips is only going up. When every major industry depends on your machines, the moat isn't just wide. It's uncrossable.

data.heading: No Second Source
data.body: AI, autonomous vehicles, quantum — all need ASML.

scenes.narration_text: So what's the play? If you're the 'lucky' type, you'll chase the next hot tip and hope for the best. But if you're the 'boring' type, you'll do what boring investors do: you'll wait for the next political headline to knock the price down, and you'll buy the most important company on Earth at a discount. Again.

But habit beats luck every single time. This company must stay on your watchlist. If the value slips again because of a political headline — just like it did in October 2024 — that is your moment to buy into the tracks of the digital age.

data.heading: Habit Beats Luck
data.body: Political dips are buying opportunities for the boring investor.

scenes.narration_text: In the Angstrom Era, they don't just participate in the market — they ARE the market. Every advanced chip, every AI model, every smartphone processor traces back to one building in Veldhoven, in the south of the Netherlands. There is no workaround. There is no plan B. There never was.

data.title: The Starting Line, the Track, the Finish Line

scenes.narration_text: Success isn't a matter of luck. It is the inevitable result of forty years of being exceptionally, relentlessly, boringly boring.

data.statement: Forty years of being exceptionally boring.

scenes.narration_text: And on that bombshell, it is time to go. Thanks for watching Luck vs. Boring. We'll see you in the next one.

data.heading: Luck vs. Boring
data.body: See you in the next one.
